buffer_this_round: When a pediatric patient is seizure‑free on a low‑dose valproate regimen, shows no toxicity, and faces adherence or cost barriers, the safest immediate recommendation is to continue the current dose rather than increase or add medications.
If a child with generalized epilepsy is seizure‑free for >12 months on valproate + carbamazepine with no toxicity, the safest choice is to continue the current regimen unchanged.

In children under 3 years with frequent focal seizures and no prior antiseizure therapy, valproate monotherapy is an appropriate first‑line choice, especially when a developmental epileptic encephalopathy (e.g., possible Dravet syndrome) is suspected.
If a medication previously documented is absent from the current prescription, assume it has been discontinued unless an explicit “continue” or dosing instruction is present.

If a child with focal epilepsy is seizure‑free on carbamazepine and shows no adverse‑effect signs, continuation of the current dose is appropriate even when the dose is below the usual therapeutic range.
If a follow‑up note reports seizure freedom and makes no explicit stop order, assume continuation of the previously prescribed antiseizure drug.
When seizure control is achieved with valproate (± clonazepam), phenobarbital should be discontinued to simplify the regimen.

When a patient’s vomiting occurs consistently with each dose of an antiseizure medication, prioritize evaluating drug intolerance and adjusting or switching the antiseizure drug before adding anti‑emetic therapy.
When a child with focal motor seizures is well‑controlled on carbamazepine but experiences occasional breakthrough seizures, adding levetiracetam as an adjunct is an appropriate management step.

When a child on valproate experiences occasional breakthrough seizures only during intercurrent illness and shows no signs of toxicity, the safest strategy is to maintain the current valproate regimen rather than adding or switching antiseizure drugs.
